title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Participant Workbook
text: 60 Institute of Child Nutrition Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Participant’s Workbook Meats/Meat Alternates Component Summary • Meet daily and weekly minimums for each grade group. • Creditable servings are measured by weight using ounce equivalents. • Combination foods may be credited for up to three different meal components (meats/ meat alternates, grains, vegetables, or fruits). • The smallest creditable amount is 0.25 oz eq.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Participant Workbook
text: 61 Institute of Child Nutrition Participant’s Workbook Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners LESSON 6: Modifying Menu Offerings for Grade Groups to Meet Meal Pattern Requirements for the National School Lunch Program Grade Groups All school lunches provide important nutrients that support learning, growth, and overall health. School lunches also have to meet average weekly amounts for calories (energy), saturated fat, so- dium (salt) and added sugars (added sugars requirement effective July 1, 2027). For example, K-5 require less calories than 9-12. This is because the different age groups require different amounts of calories. A 9–12 grade group menu can be modified by consulting the Meal Pattern Chart to meet all grade group meal pattern requirements. The NSLP Meal Pattern Chart provides the daily and weekly meal pattern components and mini- mum serving sizes for K–5, 6–8, 9–12, and K–8 grade groups. Milk Component The milk requirement is the same across all grade groups. Fruits Component The serving size for fruits for the 9–12 grade group is a minimum of 1 cup daily for a total of 5 cups per week. The serving size is ½ cup daily for a total of 2 ½ cups per week for the K–5 and 6–8 grade groups. Having the same serving size for this meal component for both grade groups is con- venient if you plan one K–8 menu. Recall that no more than half of the fruit or vegetable servings may be in the form of juice. Vegetables Component The serving size for vegetables for the 9–12 grade group is a minimum of 1 cup daily for a total of 5 cups per week. The K–5 and 6–8 grade groups require ¾ cup of vegetables daily for a total of 3 ¾ cups per week. The weekly minimum requirements are the same for the dark green beans, peas, and lentils, and starchy subgroups for all grade groups—½ cup. The weekly minimum requirement for the red/ orange and other subgroups is different between the 9–12 grade group and the younger grade groups. The 9–12 grade group requires 1¼ cups of the red/orange subgroup, whereas the K–5 and 6–8 grade groups require ¾ cup. The 9–12 grade group requires ¾ cup of the other subgroup, whereas the K–5 and 6–8 grade groups require only ½ cup.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Participant Workbook
text: 62 Institute of Child Nutrition Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Participant’s Workbook Grains Component For grains, the minimum daily requirement for the 9–12 grade group is 2 oz eq, whereas it is 1 oz eq for the K–5 and 6–8 grade groups. The 9–12 grade group minimum weekly grains requirement (10–12 oz eq) can be met by serving the minimum daily requirement (2 oz eq). That is not the case for the lower grade groups. More than the daily minimum (1 oz eq) must be offered to reach the required weekly minimums (8–9 oz eq for the K–5 grade group; 8–10 oz eq for the 6–8 grade group). The whole grain-rich criteria are the same for all grade groups. At least 80% of grains offered weekly on menus must be whole grain-rich; the remaining grain items must be enriched. For ex- ample, if serving 8 oz eq of grains across a 5-day school week, 6.5 oz eq need to be whole grain- rich to meet the 80% criteria. Recall that grain-based desserts are limited to 2 oz eq or less weekly for all grade groups. Meats/Meat Alternates Component For meats/meat alternates, the minimum daily requirement for the 9–12 grade group is 2 oz eq, whereas it is 1 oz eq for the K–5 and 6–8 grade groups. While the 9–12 grade group minimum weekly meats/meat alternates requirement (10–12 oz eq) can be met by serving the minimum daily requirement (2 oz eq), that is not the case for the lower grade groups. More than the daily minimum (1 oz eq) must be offered to reach the required weekly minimums (8–10 oz eq for the K–5 grade group; 9–10 oz eq for the 6–8 grade group). Over Versus Serve (OVS) in NSLP Under OVS, students must select three meal components to ensure they get the nutritional benefits of a meal. OVS is required for lunches served in high schools, but is optional in middle and elementary schools. OVS is not required for meals offered as part of field trips or for any other meals served away from the school campus. The required five food components must be offered for school lunch. Students must select at least three of the five required food components, including at least ½ cup of fruit and/or vegetable, to have a reimburs- able lunch. Use this simple checklist to determine if student lunches are reimbursable under OVS: • Does the meal offered to students include the minimum required amounts of vegetables, fruits, grains, meats/meat alternates, and fluid milk? • Does the meal selected by the student contain at least three components, including at least ½ cup fruit and/or vegetable? If the answer to each of these questions is yes, then the school lunch is reimbursable under OVS.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Participant Workbook
Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Participant Workbook
text: 64 Institute of Child Nutrition Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Participant’s Workbook LESSON 7: School Breakfast Program SBP Compared to NSLP The biggest difference between the lunch and breakfast meal pattern is that the breakfast meal pattern requires only three meal components: milk, fruits (or vegetables), and combined grains and meats/meat alternates. The three grade group meal patterns allow for optional overlapping of the meal component offerings, meaning the same menu and servings can potentially be offered to all three grade groups. School Breakfast Program School breakfasts must meet the applicable recommendations of the Dietary Guidelines for Amer- icans and are designed to ensure that students enter the classroom well-nourished and ready to learn. The meal pattern for breakfast includes fruits (or vegetables), whole grain-rich foods and/or meats/meat alternates, and milk. The minimum daily and weekly requirement for milk and fruits (or vegetables) is the same for all grade groups, 1 cup daily and 5 cups weekly. The minimum daily requirement for grains is 1 oz eq for all grade groups; the weekly minimum varies by grade group. The meal pattern overlaps and allows for optional K–8 and K–12 menus.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Participant Workbook
text: 65 Institute of Child Nutrition Participant’s Workbook Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Optional Components Meats/meat alternates and vegetables are not required meal components of the breakfast meal pattern but can contribute to a reimbursable meal in some instances. These foods can also be served as extras that are non-creditable and do not contribute to the reimbursable meal; however, they are counted toward the dietary specifications. Planning reimbursable meals for the School Breakfast Program follows a similar process as plan- ning menus for the National School Lunch Program. Like the National School Lunch Program, the School Breakfast Program follows a meal pattern with required components and daily minimum re- quired serving amounts to meet weekly averages. The same weekly juice and grains requirements apply at breakfast. No more than ½ of the total weekly fruits (or vegetables) component require- ments may be met with full-strength juice. Eighty percent of grains offered at breakfast must be whole grain-rich, the remaining grains must be enriched. Refer to Lesson 4 for an extensive expla- nation on how to credit grains. Grains credit the same in SBP as in NSLP. School Breakfast Program Menu Crediting Fruit Juice No more than half of the total weekly fruits may be met with full-strength fruit or vegetable juice. School Breakfast Program Menu Crediting Vegetables for Fruits Schools can continue to substitute vegetables for fruits in the SBP. Single Day Offering: Schools substituting vegetables for breakfast on one day per school week can offer any creditable vegetable, including starchy vegetables. Multiple Days Offering: Schools substituting vegetables for fruits at breakfast on two or more days per school week must offer vegetables from at least two different subgroups. Schools must choose from the following vegetable subgroups: Dark green; Red/orange; Beans, Peas, and Lentils; Starchy; and “Other” vegetables. After offering vegetables from two different subgroups, schools can choose vegetables from any subgroup, including those already offered, for the rest of the week. Vegetables can also be offered as an extra item subject to weekly dietary specifications for calo- ries, saturated fat, sodium and added sugars (added sugars effective date July 1, 2027). School Breakfast Program Menu Offering Vegetables as Extras Some programs opt to include vegetables in their breakfast menu as “extras,” meaning they do not contribute as a reimbursable component. An example of this is a hashbrown. This approach helps line staff recognize the reimbursable meal more easily if vegetables aren’t on the breakfast menu daily. Note, you are not required to include vegetables in your breakfast menu. If you decide to include vegetables, choose whether to offer it as a component or as an extra on your menu and make the appropriate changes and provide training for staff. Also, extra foods do count toward the calorie and nutrient totals.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Participant Workbook
text: 66 Institute of Child Nutrition Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Participant’s Workbook School Breakfast Program Combined Grains and Meats/Meat Alternates Component Schools can offer grains, meats/meat alternates, or a combination of both in order to meet the combined component requirement at breakfast. The minimum daily requirement is 1 oz eq and the minimum weekly requirements are 7-9 oz eq, depending on the age/grade group. Schools have the flexibility to decide the combination of grains and/or meats/meat alternates to offer at breakfast. At least 80% of the weekly grains offered at breakfast must be whole-grain rich, and the remaining grain items may be whole grain-rich or enriched. SBP and OVS Menu planners have one more task regarding breakfast menus. Under Offer Versus Serve (op- tional at all grade levels for breakfast), the daily breakfast menu must offer at least four food items from the three required meal components (fruit, combined grains and meats/meat alternates, and fluid milk). Students must select at least three of the four food items, including at least ½ cup of fruit and/or vegetable. One way to identify food items on the menu is to consistently make a milk choice 1 item, offer fruits in two ½ cup amounts so that each can be a food item, and then depending on the grains, identify the food item as either one or two items as appropriate for serving. For example, if a pre-packaged French toast product is offered, the package may be counted as 1 food item even if it credits as 1 oz eq grains and 1 oz eq meats/meat alternates. Bagels may be offered as either 1 oz eq or 2 oz eq (half or whole) so that each half counts as a food item. Half would be one item; the whole bagel would be 2 food items. A reimbursable breakfast must include ½ cup of fruit (or vegetable that credits as fruit) and two other items that credit as components. It is critical for service line staff to understand which food items on the menu are creditable and which food items are extras on the menu. The food items, all of the parts of the reimbursable meal choices, are identified by component contribution at the beginning of the service so that students can recognize a reimbursable meal. School Breakfast Program Summary In summary, the main points to remember when writing menus for the School Breakfast Program: • The breakfast meal pattern requires only three meal components: milk, fruits, and combined grains and meats/meat alternates. • Meet daily and weekly minimums for each grade group.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Participant Workbook
text: 67 Institute of Child Nutrition Participant’s Workbook Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Lunch Menu Planning Form Monday Tuesday Wednesday Thursday Friday Grains¹ Grains¹ Grains¹ Grains¹ Grains¹ ___oz eq_________ ________________ ___oz eq_________ ________________ *M/MA² ___oz eq_________ ________________ ___oz eq_________ ________________ *M/MA² ___oz eq_________ ________________ ___oz eq_________ ________________ *M/MA² ___oz eq_________ ________________ ___oz eq_________ ________________ *M/MA² ___oz eq_________ ________________ ___oz eq_________ ________________ *M/MA² Fruits³ Fruits³ Fruits³ Fruits³ Fruits³ ½ cup Diced Peachews ½ cup Mixed Berry Juice ½ cup Spicy Applesauce ½ cup Orange Juice ½ cup Orange Wedges ½ cup Grape Juice ½ cup credible Raisins (¼ c serving) ½ cup Pineapple Juice ½ cup cup Blueberries ½ cup Apple Juice Milk Milk Milk Milk Milk 1 cup FF Milk 1 cup 1% Milk 1 cup FF Milk 1 cup 1% Milk 1 cup FF Milk 1 cup 1% Milk 1 cup FF Milk 1 cup 1% Milk 1 cup FF Milk 1 cup 1% Milk Extras Extras Extras Extras Extras _________________ _________________ _________________ _________________ _________________ _________________ _________________ _________________ _________________ _________________ ¹ 80% of grains for all grade groups must be whole grain-rich. The 80% applies only to grains menu items. ² M/MA can be credited to additional weekly grains once the daily minimum of grains, 1 oz eq at for all grade groups, is met. If M/MA is credited toward additional weekly grains the 80% whole grain-rich applies only to grains. ³ Fruits are limited to no more than half (50%) of the total Fruits offered during the weekly can be 100%, pasteurized full-strength juice. Vegetables can be credited toward fruits requirements when specific criteria is met. Vegetables may substitute for fruits at breakfast when the first two cups per week belong to the dark green, red/orange, beans and peas, or other vegetable subgroups. Vegetables may also be served as Extras that do not credit toward the reimbursable meal, but do count in nutrient targets for calories, saturated and trans fat limits, and grade group specific sodium targets.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Participant Workbook
text: 68 Institute of Child Nutrition Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Participant’s Workbook LESSON 8: Nutritional Standards The primary goal of FBMP is to assist SFAs in planning menus that meet the nutrition goals when averaged over a school week. The NSLP and SBP have several nutrition goals for menus, including: • Approximately one-third of the daily requirements at lunch and one-fourth of the daily requirements at breakfast for total calories and more than 25 key nutrients. • Limits on calories and nutrients often consumed in excess o Saturated fat o Sodium o Trans fat Calories Students need calories to fuel growth, development, learning, and physical activity. However, many American children consume more calories than they need. Often, the calories they consume are not rich in nutrients. School meals are designed to provide abundant nutrients with sufficient calories. The amounts listed on the Meal Pattern Chart are a range of calories to be offered each day when averaged over the 5-day week. Follow the meal pattern guidelines, and the meals you plan are likely to meet the calorie range goals. Good menu planning includes balance and a variety of foods; the meal patterns are built on the principles of good menu planning. If you discover that weekly menus are too high in calories, consider these tips: • Choose lean meats. • Choose fewer processed foods. • Limit condiments or use ones that are lower in calories. • Prepare scratch or speed-scratch condiments and spice blends help limit sodium and calories. • Offer a rainbow of vegetables and fruits with no added sugars. • Limit grain-based desserts. For menus too low in calories, offer the higher end of weekly ounce equivalents for grains and meats/meat alternates. Saturated Fat Saturated fat is often found in forms that are solid at room temperature – examples include milk fat, butter, or the fat inside or around meat. A few food products such as coconut oil, palm oil, or whole milk remain as liquids at room temperature but are high in saturated fat. Limiting saturated fat and trans fats in school meals helps reduce the risk of children developing heart disease later in life. The meal patterns emphasize foods that are naturally low in or free of saturated fats. To limit the amount of saturated fats in your menu, choose lower-fat and lean dairy, meat, and poultry

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Participant Workbook
text: 69 Institute of Child Nutrition Participant’s Workbook Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners options—like skim milk, lean beef, grilled chicken breast without the skin, or plant-based meals, like veggie burgers. Calories from saturated fat must average less than 10% of the total calories of the weekly menu. Trans Fat Dietary specifications prohibiting synthetic trans fat in the school lunch and breakfast programs and in foods sold to children on campus during the school day have been removed. Sodium Sodium is a mineral that is essential in small quantities. It helps control your body’s fluid balance, sends nerve impulses, and affects muscle function. However, most people—including children— consume too much sodium. Planning menus that are lower in sodium means choosing fewer processed foods and incorporating the use of fresh or frozen vegetables. When planning for canned vegetables, choose ones with lower salt content. USDA Foods provides a variety of low sodium and no salt added canned vegetables and other products. Select sauces and condiments with care. For example, soy sauce, bottled salad dressings, dips, ketchup, jarred salsas, mustard, pickles, olives, and relish can be high in sodium. Look for reduced- or lower-sodium versions. You can also prepare your own sauces and condiments to help reduce sodium. Choose recipes that enhance flavor with a variety of spices rather than salt. See the resource in your materials Sodium Content by Meal component (excerpt from the Menu Planner for School Meals) to review how each component may contribute to the total sodium of a meal. Check out ICN’s Shaking It Up: Small Changes Lead to Big Flavors (https://theicn.org/shaking-it- up/) series of worksheets and online courses for more sodium reduction strategies. Added Sugars Added sugars include sugars added during processing of foods (such as sucrose or dextrose), foods packaged as sweeteners (such as table sugar), sugars from syrups and honey, and sugars from concentrated fruit or vegetable juices. They do not include naturally occurring sugars in milks, fruits, and vegetables. To meet DGAs, there is a Two-Step Approach to Reduce Added Sugars in School Meals Step 1: Implement product-based limits for breakfast cereals, yogurt and flavored milk by SY 2025–2026 (effective date July 1, 2025). Step 2: Implement a weekly dietary limit for added sugars to less than 10% of calories per week by SY 2027–2028 (effective date July 1, 2027).

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Participant Workbook
text: 70 Institute of Child Nutrition Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Participant’s Workbook Non-Creditable Foods Non-creditable foods are foods and beverages that cannot credit in USDA’s meal patterns for Child Nutrition Programs. They include foods and beverages in amounts too small to credit (i.e., less than the minimum creditable amount) and foods and beverages that do not belong to the meal pattern components. Examples include potato chips, pudding, ice cream, gelatin, cream cheese, bacon, and condiments. SFAs may serve non-creditable foods in addition to the meal components to add variety, help improve acceptability in the meal, and satisfy appetites. Examples include maple syrup on pancakes, salad dressing on tossed greens, and condiments such as ketchup or mustard on sandwiches and other entrees. Non-creditable foods typically contain few nutrients and are higher in added sugars, saturated fats, and sodium. Menu planners should read labels, be aware of the ingredients in foods, and limit the frequency and amount of less nutritious choices. Non-creditable foods offered as part of reimbursable meals for grades K–12 must count toward the weekly nutrient standards for school meals and cannot cause the menu to exceed the average weekly limits for calories, saturated fat, sodium and added sugars (added sugars effective date July 1, 2027). Nutrient Analysis There are different ways to analyze the week’s menu. You can choose your preferred method, which includes USDA-approved nutrient analysis software, spreadsheets, or paper and pen counts. Whatever you use, you are annually required to attest that your menu meets all the components, subgroups, and nutrient specifications. Certification of Compliance Worksheets are provided by USDA. You can find it on the USDA website and most likely on your State agency’s website. You simply enter your daily menus, and it calculates the meal component requirements. It is color-coded; green shows requirements that are met, and red shows requirements that are over or under. The simplified nutrient assessment tab (last one in the worksheet) calculates weekly averages for dietary specs. It shows if calories, sodium, sat fat are within ranges. The simplified nutrient assessment is not completely accurate as it only takes into account entree, side dish, and condiment nutrient specs, but it’s a good estimate of dietary specifications. Your district may have access to meal planning software that performs the same function. Technical Assistance & Guidance If you need additional help with food based menu planning, contact your State agency. Email helpdesk@theicn.org or call the ICN Help Desk at 1-800-321-3054.

text: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners

text: Ice Breaker

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Reimbursable Meal Training for Menu Planning Food Based Menu Planning (FBMP): Uses meal patterns and grade groups as planning tools. Reimbursable Meal: A single meal unit that offers all of the USDA daily required meal pattern components for each age/grade group served in the minimum required amounts.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Training Objectives Identify the fluid milk component choices, serving size requirements, and how to credit fluid milk toward meeting meal pattern requirements for the National School Lunch Program (NSLP). Identify the fruits component choices, serving size requirements, and how to credit fruits toward meeting meal pattern requirements for the NSLP. Identify the vegetables component choices, serving size requirements, and how to credit vegetables toward meeting meal pattern requirements for the NSLP. Identify the grains component choices, serving size requirements, and how to credit grains toward meeting meal pattern requirements for the NSLP. Identify the meats/meat alternates component choices, serving size requirements, and how to credit meats/meat alternates toward meeting meal pattern requirements for the NSLP. Modify menu offerings for grade groups K–5 and 6–8 to meet meal pattern requirements for the NSLP. Identify the meal components and serving size requirements to meet the School Breakfast Program meal pattern requirements. Define nutrient standards in relation to the meal pattern requirements for the National School Lunch Program and School Breakfast Program.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Show up on time. Be present. Let everyone participate. Listen with an open mind. Think before speaking. Attack the problem not the person. GROUND

text: Key Terms Refer to the key terms chart in your Participant’s Workbook

text: Meal Components M ilk Fruits Vegetables Grains Meats/Meat Alternates

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Recognizable Versus Unrecognizable Meal Components Recognizable Food Item: a food item visible in the breakfast or lunch offered that allows students to identify the food groups and amounts recommended for consumption at mealtime

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Dietary Guidelines for Americans, 2020–2025 Revised every five years Meal patterns may continue to change to align with the guidelines

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Overview of Offer Versus Serve (OVS) A provision in NSLP and SBP that allows students to decline some of the food offered. The goals are to reduce food waste in the school meals programs while permitting students to decline foods they do not intend to eat.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: OVS at Lunch Must offer students all 5 required food components in at least the minimum required amounts Must take at least 3 components in the required serving sizes One selection must be at least ½ cup from either the fruit or vegetable component Required at the high school level

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: OVS at Breakfast Must offer students all 3 required food components in at least the minimum required amounts Must be offered at least 4 food items Must select at least 3 food items, one of which must be ½ cup of fruit or vegetables for OVS OVS is optional at all grade levels for breakfast

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Lesson 1: Milk Component for the National School Lunch Program

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Milk Component Many health benefits Important for building and maintaining strong bones Provides nutrients including calcium, potassium, vitamin D, and protein

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Lesson Overview and Objective Identify the fluid milk component choices, serving size requirements, and how to credit fluid milk toward meeting meal pattern requirements for the NSLP.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Fluid Milk Requirements Must have vitamins A and D at levels specified by the FDA 2 levels of milk fat are allowed as options for all grade groups: Fat-free milk (also called nonfat or skim) 1% low-fat milk

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Fluid Milk Requirements (cont’d) Fat-free and 1% low-fat milk: Unflavored Flavored 2 different options must be offered 1 is required to be unflavored

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Milk Component Daily Required Minimum Serving Size Grade Group Daily Minimum Weekly Minimum K–5 , 6 – 8 , 9 – 12 1 cup (8 fl oz) 5 cups

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Milk Component Requirements and Allowable Options Unflavored 1% milk and flavored fat-free milk as standard options Offering an unflavored option at every meal is required

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: ACTIVITY: Milk Component – Menu Planning Form

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Milk Component Section of Food Buying Guide

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Creditable Types of Milk The Food Buying Guide lists other types of pasteurized milk, such as: Lactose-free Buttermilk Ultra-High Temperature (UHT) milk, both unflavored and flavored, fat-free and 1%

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Nondairy Milk Substitutes MUST Be Nutritionally Equivalent Nondairy beverages may be served in place of fluid milk Must meet these specific nutrition requirements

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Nondairy Milk Substitute Nutrition Standards Nutrient Requirement per Cup (8 fl oz) Protein 8 gm Calcium 276 mg Vitamin A 150 mcg Vitamin D 2.5 mcg Magnesium 24 mg Phosphorus 222 mg Potassium 349 mg Riboflavin 0.44 mg Vitamin B12 1.1 mcg

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Offering Nondairy Milk Substitutes Offer nutritionally equivalent fortified soy milk with other fluid milk options without requiring a non-disability special dietary request for a milk substitute Other plant-based beverages, such as oat, rice, or pea, are usually not nutritionally equivalent for all the nutrients listed

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Resources for Accommodating Children with Special Dietary Needs

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Milk Component Wrap Up What did you learn about planning menus that meet the meal pattern requirements for the milk component?

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Milk Component Summary Provide 1 cup (8 fl oz) serving size for all grade groups, at both lunch and breakfast Include at least two choices at each meal, at least one choice must be unflavored. Choose from unflavored or flavored fat-free and 1% milk. Follow the substitution instructions if milk alternates are offered for non-disability reasons.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Lesson 2: Fruits Component for the National School Lunch Program

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Benefits of Fruits Fruits are sources of many essential nutrients such as: Potassium Dietary fiber Vitamin C Folate

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Lesson Overview and Objective Identify the fruits component choices, serving size requirements, and how to credit fruits toward meeting meal pattern requirements for the NSLP.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Favorite Fruits Activity Time!

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Fruits Component Requirements Grade Group Daily Minimum Weekly Minimum K–5 , 6 – 8 ½ cup 2 ½ cups 9–12 1 cup 5 cups

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Crediting Fruits Measured in volume Must be adequate and recognizable to meet the meal pattern requirements Minimum creditable serving size for fruits is ⅛ cup

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Types of Fruits Fresh Frozen Canned Dried 100% Juice There are over 360 entries for fruits in the FBG.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Fresh Fruit Comes in a variety of colors, shapes and sizes Understand what a creditable serving size is and be able to communicate that to those who prepare and serve meals Fresh fruit when possible is the best choice for students

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Canned Fruit Select fruit canned in 100% fruit juice, water, or light syrup Canned fruit may include the juice or liquid syrup in which the fruit is packed

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Frozen Fruit A serving of thawed frozen fruit consists of fruit plus the juice or liquid that accumulated during thawing Offer all year that may not grow in your region or are only available fresh when in season

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Dried Fruit Whole dried fruit and whole dried fruit pieces credit at twice the volume served For example: ⅛ cup of dried cranberries = ¼ cup fruit Choose options with no added sugar

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Juice To be creditable, juices must be pasteurized, 100% fruit juice Fruit juice is limited to half or less of the fruits planned for the week Juice cannot be credited when used as an ingredient in another food or beverage product with the exception of smoothies

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Non-Creditable Fruit Products Products that do not count as fruit: Snack-type foods made from fruits, such as fried banana chips Pickle relish, jam, or jelly

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Increasing Fruit Consumption How can we incorporate more fruits into menu planning and increase student consumption?

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Activity: Fruits Component – Menu Planning Form

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Fruits Component Wrap Up What did you learn about planning menus that meet the meal pattern requirements for the fruit component?

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Fruits Component Summary At lunch, provide at least ½ cup daily for grades K-5 and 6-8, and 1 cup daily for grades 9-12. The smallest creditable amount for the fruits component is 1/8 cup. Credit dried fruits at twice the volume (1/8 cup credits as ¼ cup). Limit 100% fruit juice to half or less of fruits component weekly, including fruits credited in smoothies.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Lesson 3: Vegetables Component for the National School Lunch Program

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Benefits of Vegetables Vegetables are important sources of many nutrients including potassium, dietary fiber, folate, vitamin A and vitamin C.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Lesson Overview and Objective Identify the vegetables component choices, serving size requirements, and how to credit vegetables toward meeting meal pattern requirements for the NSLP.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Favorite Vegetables Activity Time!

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Vegetables Component Must be fresh, frozen, canned, dried (including dried legumes), or 100% full-strength vegetable juice The FBG has over 630 entries for vegetables

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Vegetables Component Serving Size Minimum creditable amount is 1/8 cup Grade Group Daily Minimum Weekly Minimum K–5 , 6 – 8 ¾ cup 3 ¾ cups 9–12 1 cup 5 cups

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Vegetable Subgroups Dark green Red/orange Beans and peas (legumes) Starchy Other vegetables

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Weekly Vegetable Subgroup Requirements Vegetable Subgroup Weekly Minimum K – 5, 6 – 8 Weekly Minimum 9 – 12 Dark Green* ½ cup ½ cup Red/Orange ¾ cup 1¼ cup Bean s, Peas, and Lentils ½ cup ½ cup Starchy ½ cup ½ cup Other** ½ cup ¾ cup Subgroup Totals 2 ¾ cups 3 ½ cups Additional Vegetables 1 cup 1 ½ cups Total Weekly Requirement 3 ¾ cups 5 cups *Raw leafy greens (including iceberg lettuce) credit for half the volume; ¼ cup credits as ⅛ cup. **Other vegetable subgroup requirement may be met with any additional amounts from the dark green, red/orange, and beans and peas vegetable subgroups.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Crediting Vegetables The Food Buying Guide is an essential resource for crediting fresh, frozen, canned, dried vegetables, and 100% vegetable juice Helpful feature is the identification of vegetables by subgroup

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Crediting Vegetables (cont’d) Raw leafy greens credit at half the volume served For example, 1 cup of chopped romaine lettuce credits as ½ cup toward the dark green vegetable subgroup Cooked leafy greens, such as collard greens, are credited by volume as served For example, ½ cup of cooked collard greens credits as ½ cup of dark green vegetables

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Sample Menu for Vegetables Daily & Weekly Required Serving Amounts Monday Tuesday Wednesday Thursday Friday ½ cup Broccoli ½ cup Carrots ¾ cup Tossed Salad (½ c iceberg lettuce, ¼ c red pepper and tomato) ½ cup Cauliflower ½ cup Celery and Jicama Sicks ½ cup Acorn Squash ½ cup Fries ½ cup Refried Beans ½ cup Green Beans ½ cup Carrots

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Activity: Vegetables Component – Menu Planning Form

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Food Buying Guide for Vegetables

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: ICN ’ s iLearn Food Buying Guide Series

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Crediting Specific to Vegetables Beans, peas, and lentils refers to dry, mature beans, lentils, and split peas (canned, dried, or frozen) Credit toward the vegetables component OR meats/meat alternates component Only credit toward one food component in a menu item, not both

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Crediting Specific to Vegetables (cont’d) Vegetable juice must be pasteurized, 100% vegetable juice Blends containing vegetables from the same subgroup may contribute toward that vegetable subgroup Blends containing vegetables from more than one subgroup may credit as additional vegetables

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Activity:Menu Sample for Vegetables Daily, Weekly, and Subgroup Weekly Required Serving Amounts Monday Tuesday Wednesday Thursday Friday ½ cup Broccoli ½ cup Carrots ¾ cup Tossed Salad (½ c iceberg lettuce, ¼ c red pepper and tomato) ½ cup Cauliflower ½ cup Celery and Jicama Sicks ½ cup Acorn Squash ½ cup Fries ½ cup Refried Beans ½ cup Green Beans ½ cup Carrots Subgroups: ½ c dark green, ½ c red/orange ½ c red/orange, ½ c starchy ¼ c other, ½ c red/orange, ½ c legumes 1 c other ½ c other, ¼ c starchy, ½ c red/orange

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Activity: Vegetable Subgroups on Menu *Raw leafy greens (including iceberg lettuce) credit for half the volume; ½ cup credits as ¼ cup.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Salad Bar Example Offering a salad bar that meets the daily and weekly minimum serving amounts

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Vegetables Component Wrap Up What did you learn about planning menus that meet the meal pattern requirements for the vegetables component?

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Vegetables Component Summary At lunch, provide ¾ cup daily for grades K–5, 6–8, and 1 cup daily for grades 9–12. Meet the weekly subgroup requirements. Credit raw leafy greens at half the volume. Credit beans, peas, and lentils as vegetable subgroup or as meats/meat alternates, but not both for the same menu item. Limit 100% vegetable juice to half or less of vegetables component weekly, including vegetables credited in smoothies. The smallest creditable amount for the vegetables component is 1/8 cup.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Lesson 4: Grains Component for the National School Lunch Program

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Benefits of Grains Contribute energy, dietary fiber, iron, B vitamins, and other nutrients Whole grains are a source of nutrients, such as dietary fiber, iron, zinc, manganese, folate, magnesium, copper, thiamin, niacin, vitamin B6, phosphorus, selenium, riboflavin and vitamin A

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Lesson Overview and Objective Identify the grains component choices, serving size requirements, and how to credit grains toward meeting meal pattern requirements for the NSLP.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Grains Component Requirements

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Grains: Types of Ingredients Whole grains Enriched grains Bran or germ

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Grains: Food Examples Breads, biscuits, bagels, rolls, tortillas, crackers, and cereal grains (cooked) Ready-to-eat (RTE) breakfast cereals Cereals or bread products Macaroni, pasta, noodle products (cooked) Grain-based desserts Non-sweet snack food products

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Grains: Daily and Weekly Required Minimums Grade Group Daily Minimum Weekly Minimum K–5 1 oz eq 8–9 oz eq 6–8 1 oz eq 8–10 oz eq 9–12 2 oz eq 10–12 oz eq

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Grain-Based Desserts Can be included at lunch May contribute up to 2 oz eq towards the grains requirement each week Exhibit A denotes specific grain products that credit as grain-based desserts May be whole grain-rich or made with enriched grains Count toward the weekly dietary specifications

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Crediting Criteria for Grains Component Whole-grain flour Whole-grain meal Corn masa, masa harina , hominy Enriched flour Enriched meal, bran, germ, Enriched product, such as enriched bread, or a fortified cereal

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Crediting Criteria for Grains Component (cont'd) Oz eq are used to determine the amount of creditable grains ¼ ounce equivalent (0.25 oz eq ) is the smallest amount allowable to be credited toward the grains requirement

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Crediting Criteria for Grains Component (cont'd) At least 80% of the weekly grains offered must meet the whole grain-rich criteria 100% whole grain Contain a blend of whole-grain ingredients and enriched grain ingredients of which at least 50% is whole grain

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Crediting Criteria for Grains Component (cont'd) Up to 20% of the grain items offered weekly can be made from grains that are enriched meal and/or flour Bran and germ can be used to meet the enriched grains requirements

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Crediting Grains Example For the 9–12 grade group meal pattern, a minimum of 10 oz eq of grains per week are required. If the minimum servings are offered on the menu, 8 of the 10 servings must be whole grain-rich. The other 2 servings must be enriched.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Food Buying Guide for Child Nutrition Programs Exhibit A Breads, cereals, muffins, crackers, pasta, etc. all contribute differently to the grains requirement based on the weight of each product. The Food Buying Guide for Child Nutrition Programs’ Exhibit A (Attachment A) provides a general guideline for crediting prepared grain items.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Exhibit A Group B and Group E Examples

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Exhibit A Group H and Group I Examples Volume measure is included for items usually served by volume Provides information on dry product yields for ounce equivalents for grains For grain items in Group H of Exhibit A, the volumes or weights listed in the chart can be used to meet grains requirements.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Other Crediting Information for Grains

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Whole Grain-Rich Criteria 80% of the amount of grains served per week must be whole grain-rich Contain 100% whole grain or at least 50% whole grain meal and/or flour Any remaining meal/flour must be enriched, bran, or germ

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Whole Grain-Rich Criteria (cont'd) Groups A-G, the whole- grain content per oz eq must be at least 8.0 grams or more Groups H and I, the whole-grain content must be at least half of the volume or weight listed in the chart Child Nutrition (CN) label to indicate the oz eq of grains in a food product.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Whole Grain-Rich Criteria (cont'd) “Diets rich in whole grain foods and other plant foods, and low in total fat, saturated fat, and cholesterol, may reduce the risk of heart disease and certain cancers.” OR “Diets rich in whole grain foods and other plant foods, and low in saturated fat and cholesterol, may help reduce the risk of heart disease.”

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Whole Grain-Rich Criteria (cont'd) Whole grains are the primary grain ingredient by weight. Specifically: Non-mixed dishes (e.g., breads, cereals Mixed dishes (e.g., pizza, corn dogs)

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Whole Grain-Rich Criteria (cont'd) State agency’s Special Supplemental Nutrition Program for Women, Infants, and Children (WIC)-approved whole-grain food list

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Activity: Grains Component – Menu Planning Form

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Grains Menu Choices Monday Tuesday Wednesday Thursday Friday Pasta Bun Tortilla Rice Pizza Crust

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Whole Grain-Rich Criteria – Special Circumstances If you plan menus with additional grains, you must ensure the 80% criteria is maintained.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Grains Component Wrap Up What did you learn about planning menus that meet the meal pattern requirements for the grains component?

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Grains Component Summary Meet daily minimums for each grade group. Determine that at least 80% of credited grains are whole grain-rich. Meet weekly minimums for each grade group. Limit grain-based desserts to 2 oz eq or less weekly for all grade groups (lunch). The smallest creditable amount for the grains component is ¼ oz eq.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Lesson 5: Meats/Meat Alternates Component for the National School Lunch Program

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Benefits of Meats/Meat Alternates Include animal and plant-based foods, which are good protein sources Provide iron, zinc, magnesium, and B-vitamins.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Lesson Overview and Objective Identify the meats/meat alternates component choices, serving size requirements, and how to credit meats/meat alternates toward meeting meal pattern requirements for the NSLP.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Meats/Meat Alternates Component Measured in oz eq. Amount of the food that represents 1 ounce of edible portion of lean meat without the bone Includes: Fresh and frozen meats Processed meats Canned meats Meat alternates

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Meats/Meat Alternates Component Serving Amount of Food per Week (minimum per day) Grades K – 5 Grades 6 – 8 Grades 9 – 12 8 – 10 (1) 9 – 10 (1) 10 – 12 (2) Note: SFAs may exceed the weekly maximum for M/MA, provided that meals (on average) meet the weekly dietary specifications for calories, saturated fat, sodium, and added sugars.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Crediting Meats/Meat Alternates Use manufacturer’s documentation of the product (i.e. CN label, signed product formulation statement, or USDA Foods Fact Sheet) Use the Food Buying Guide Note: The minimum creditable amount for meats/meat alternates is 0.25 oz eq.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Crediting Nuts and Seeds Operators can offer nuts and seeds to meet the full meats/meat alternates component Or use them for only part of the component, paired with another meat/meat alternate

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Child Nutrition ( CN ) Label

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Beans, Peas, and Lentils Credit towards the beans, peas, and lentils subgroup of the vegetables component OR M/MA component Not both

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Combination Foods Single serving that contains more than one food item from different food components that cannot be separated Pizza, soup, casseroles, burritos, sandwiches Often featured as the entrée

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Activity: Meats/Meat Alternates Component – Menu Planning Form

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Meats/Meat Alternates Component Wrap Up What did you learn about planning menus that meet the meal pattern requirements for the meats/meat alternates component?

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Meats/Meat Alternates Component Summary Meet daily and weekly minimums for each grade group. Creditable servings are measured by weight using ounce equivalents. Combination foods may be credited for up to three (3) different meal components (meats/meat alternates, grains, vegetables, or fruits). The smallest creditable amount is 0.25 oz eq.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Lesson 6: Modifying Menu Offerings for Grade Groups to Meet Meal Pattern Requirements for the National School Lunch Program

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Why Grade Groups? All school lunches provide important nutrients that support learning, growth and overall health. School lunches also have to meet average weekly amounts for calories (energy), saturated fat, and sodium (salt). Different age groups have different nutrient needs. The meal patterns follow DGAs.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Lesson Overview and Objective Modify menu offerings for grade groups K–5 and 6–8 to meet meal pattern requirements for the National School Lunch Program.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Grade Groups Grades K–5 Grades 6–8 Grades 9–12

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Activity: Milk Component Grade Group Daily Minimum Weekly Minimum K–5 , 6 – 8 , 9 – 12 1 cup (8 fl oz) 5 cups

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Activity: Fruits Component Grade Group Daily Minimum Weekly Minimum K–5 , 6 – 8 ½ cup 2 ½ cups 9–12 1 cup 5 cups

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Activity: Vegetables Component Grade Group Daily Minimum Weekly Minimum K–5 , 6 – 8 ¾ cup 3 ¾ cups 9–12 1 cup 5 cups Note the specific subgroup requirements

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Activity: Grains Component Grade Group Daily Minimum Weekly Minimum K–5 1 oz eq 8–9 oz eq 6–8 1 oz eq 8–10 oz eq 9–12 2 oz eq 10–12 oz eq

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Activity: Meats/Meat Alternates Component Grade Group Daily Minimum Weekly Minimum K–5 1 oz eq 8–10 oz eq 6–8 1 oz eq 9–10 oz eq 9–12 2 oz eq 10–12 oz eq

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Over Versus Serve (OVS) in NSLP Required for lunches served in high schools, but is optional in middle and elementary schools The required five food components must be offered for school lunch. Must select at least three of the five required food components, including at least ½ cup of fruit and/or vegetable

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: OVS in NSLP (cont’d) Use this simple checklist to determine if student lunches are reimbursable under OVS: Does the meal offered to students include the minimum required amounts of vegetables, fruits, grains, meats/meat alternates, and fluid milk? Does the meal selected by the student contain at least three components, including at least ½ cup fruit and/or vegetable?

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Other Grade Groups Wrap Up What questions do you have about applying the meal pattern requirements for the multiple grade groups?

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Lesson 7: School Breakfast Program

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: SBP Compared to NSLP

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Lesson Overview and Objective Identify the meal pattern component choices and serving size requirements that meet the School Breakfast Program meal pattern requirements.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: School Breakfast Program Similar process to planning menus for NSLP Required meal components Daily required minimum serving amounts to meet weekly averages The main difference: only requires 3 meal components Milk, Fruits, & Combined Grains and Meats/Meat Alternates

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: School Breakfast Program Meal Pattern

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Planning a Breakfast Menu Activity Time!

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: School Breakfast Program Menu Crediting Fruits Menu shows ONE way to meet the fruit juice weekly limit of no more than 50% of fruit offered, Vegetables may be substituted for fruits. If substituting vegetables at breakfast on one day per school week, any creditable vegetable, including starchy vegetables, can be offered. If substituting vegetables for fruits at breakfast on two or more days per school week, vegetables from at least two different subgroups must be offered.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: School Breakfast Program Menu Crediting Vegetables for Fruits

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: School Breakfast Program Menu Offering Vegetables as Extras Some opt to include vegetables in their breakfast menu as “extras” Hashbrown . Helps line staff recognize the reimbursable meal more easily

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: School Breakfast Program Combined Grains and Meats/Meat Alternates Component Either plan another 1 oz eq of grains OR You can substitute 1 oz eq of meats/meat alternates for 1 oz eq of grains

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Grains and Meats/Meat Alternates Component Tip Tip for meeting the 80% whole grain-rich criteria: Only one ounce equivalent of the daily planned menu items is an enriched product

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: School Breakfast Program Offer Versus Serve Optional at all grade levels for breakfast Must offer at least 4 food items from the three required meal components Must select at least 3 of the 4 food items Including at least ½ cup of fruit and/or vegetable

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: School Breakfast Program Wrap Up What did you learn about planning menus that meet the meal pattern requirements for the School Breakfast Program?

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: School Breakfast Program Summary The main points to remember when writing menus for SBP: The breakfast meal pattern requires only three meal components: milk, fruits, and combined grains and meats/meat alternates. Meet daily and weekly minimums for each grade group.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Lesson 8: Nutrient Standards

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Nutrient Standards Food-based approach ensures a variety of foods and nutrients are offered each week The items to monitor include: Calories Saturated fat Sodium Added Sugars

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Lesson Overview and Objective Define nutrient standards in relation to the meal pattern requirements for the National School Lunch Program and School Breakfast Program.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Meal Pattern Chart

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Calories Amounts listed are a range of calories to be offered each day when averaged over the 5-day week No daily minimum or maximum, there is a weekly average minimum/maximum

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Tips for Calorie Management Choose lean meats Choose fewer processed foods Limit condiments, or use ones that are lower in calories Substitute hummus for a high calorie dip Offer a rainbow of vegetable and fruits with no added sugars Limit grain-based desserts

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Saturated Fat Most come from animal products, like dairy, meat, and poultry Choose lower-fat and lean options of dairy, meat, and poultry Skim milk, lean beef, and grilled chicken breast without the skin or serve plant-based proteins Calories from saturated fat must average less than 10% of the total calories of the weekly menu.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Sodium/Salt Essential in small quantities Children (and most people) consume too much sodium

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Tips for Sodium Management Planning menus that are lower in sodium means choosing whole, minimally processed foods, incorporating the use of fresh or frozen vegetables, and the use of herbs, citrus, spices, and other flavors. Select sauces and condiments with care. For example, soy sauce, bottled salad dressings, dips, ketchup, jarred salsas, capers, mustard, pickles, olives and relish can be high in sodium. Look for a reduced- or lower-sodium versions. Choose recipes that enhance flavor with a variety of spices rather than salt. Check out ICN’s Shaking It Up: Small Changes Lead to Big Flavors series of worksheets and online course for more sodium reduction strategies.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Added Sugars Two-Step Approach to Reduce Added Sugars in School Meals Step 1: Implement product-based limits for breakfast cereals, yogurt and flavored milk by SY 2025-2026 (effective date July 1, 2025). Step 2: Implement a weekly dietary limit for added sugars to less than 10% of calories per week by SY 2027-2028 (effective date July 1, 2027).

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Non-Creditable Foods Non-creditable foods are foods and beverages that cannot credit in USDA’s meal patterns for Child Nutrition Programs Potato chips, pudding, ice cream, gelatin, cream cheese, bacon, condiments (e.g., syrup, jam, ketchup, mustard, mayonnaise, and butter), and water

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Non-Creditable Foods (cont’d) SFAs may serve non-creditable foods in addition to the meal components to add variety, help improve acceptability in the meal, and satisfy appetites Count toward the weekly dietary specifications (nutrition standards) for school meals Cannot cause the menu to exceed average weekly limits for calories, saturated fat, sodium and added sugars (Added sugars effective date July 1, 2027)

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Nutrient Standards Wrap Up/Summary THINK–PAIR–SHARE Activity

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Certification of Compliance Worksheets: 5-Day Schedule This worksheet is provided by USDA. You can find it online, and most likely it is a link from your State agency’s webpage. You will enter the daily menu, including additional or non-creditable foods, and it calculates all the requirements It is color coded with green showing when certain specifications are met, and red showing that the menu is over or under in a required area.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Technical Assistance & Guidance For technical assistance with food based menu planning contact your state agency. Most have classes or webinars to explain how to use these tools that check for the calories and nutrients.

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Questions?

title: Planning Reimbursable School Meals for Menu Planners Slides
text: Post-Assessment

title: Post-Assessment
text: 1 Food Safety in Schools Post-Assessment Post-Assessment 1. Which of the following statements is true about food safety? a. School nutrition employees have no control over the safety of food served. b. Children are young and healthy, so they are not likely to get foodborne illnesses. c. A food safety program must be maintained in all school buildings. d. It is difficult to follow good food handling techniques. 2. A foodborne illness outbreak is said to occur when: a. one or more people become ill after eating the same food. b. two or more people become ill after eating the same food. c. five or more people become ill after eating the same food. d. at least 10 people become ill after eating the same food. 3. What is one important method for preventing biological hazards? a. Clean and sanitize food contact surfaces. b. Store chemicals in a separate storage area. c. Wear disposable plastic gloves over bandages on hands. d. Remove all nail polish before working with food. 4. What is one important method for preventing chemical hazards? a. Avoid wearing jewelry that could fall into food. b. Control time that food is in the temperature danger zone. c. T est sanitizing solutions to make sure they are at correct concentrations. d. Wash hands after coughing or sneezing. 5. Which of the following temperature ranges represents the temperature danger zone? a. 35 °F - 140 °F b. 50 °F - 70 °F c. 41 °F - 135 °F d. 50 °F - 140 °F

title: Post-Assessment
text: 2 Post-Assessment Food Safety in Schools 6. Which of the following practices has been identified as a main cause of foodborne illnesses? a. Purchasing food from approved sources b. Using written Standard Operating Procedures c. Abusing the time-temperature relationship d. Checking temperatures of food during cooking 7. Cross contamination can occur when: a. ready-to-eat meats are stored on the top shelf in the refrigerator. b. hands are washed between tasks. c. color-coded cutting boards are used. d. a new can of peaches is added to leftover peaches and put on the salad bar. 8. How long should employees wash their hands? a. 8 seconds b. 20 seconds c. 30 seconds d. 40 seconds 9. Which of the following statements is true about taking food temperatures? a. A bimetallic stemmed thermometer may be used for taking temperatures of hamburger patties. b. Infrared thermometers can be used for taking cooking temperatures. c. Thermometers rarely need to be calibrated. d. T emperatures should be taken in multiple locations for foods such as casseroles. 10. Which of the following statements is true about thermometer calibration? a. Thermometers should be calibrated each time they are dropped. b. Thermometers should be calibrated at least monthly. c. Thermometers do not need to be calibrated because they are guaranteed to be accurate. d. Cold water calibration is the only acceptable method.

title: Post-Assessment
text: 3 Food Safety in Schools Post-Assessment 11. Bacteria that can be harmful are called: a. spoilage bacteria. b. pathogens. c. parasites. d. viruses. 12. Which of the following actions will kill harmful bacteria? a. Freezing the food for 4 hours b. Heating the food at a low temperature for a long time c. Keeping the food out of the temperature danger zone d. Cooking the food to the required internal temperature for 15 seconds 13. What is the most common cause of foodborne illness? a. Bacteria b. Mold c. Virus d. Yeast 14. Which bacteria could cause hemolytic uremic syndrome in young children? a. Campylobacter jejuni b. Shiga toxin-producing Escherichia coli (STEC) c. Listeria monocytogenes d. Staphylococcus aureus 15. If you observe mold on food, what recommendation should you follow? a. Cut away the moldy area and use the remainder of the food. b. Discard the food. c. Use the food in something that will be cooked. d. Use the food if the mold spots are small.

title: Post-Assessment
text: 4 Post-Assessment Food Safety in Schools 16. Which of the following practices supports pest infestation and growth in a school nutrition facility? a. Removing all cardboard boxes from the facility b. Cleaning grease traps often c. Storing food items on the floor d. Installing air doors at entrances 17. A three-compartment sink should be set up and used properly. Which statement is true about using a three-compartment sink? a. Set up compartments to wash, rinse, and sanitize. b. Set up compartments to rinse, wash, and sanitize. c. Prepare sanitizing solution daily. d. Use any water temperature for mixing detergent and sanitizer. 18. What type of thermometer can be used to test final rinse temperatures? a. Meat thermometer b. Self-adhering temperature sensing label c. Infrared d. Bi-metallic stemmed thermometer 19. When using a chemical dish machine, which of the following statements is true? a. Chemical solution concentrations are different than used for manual sanitizing. b. Sanitizing solution should be tested at the end of a rinse cycle. c. T esting of sanitizing solution is not necessary because the dish machine is serviced routinely. d. It is not necessary to document chemical sanitizer concentration. 20. Which one of the following provides important information about the safety of using a chemical? a. Standard Operating Procedures b. T raining manual in school district c. Safety Data Sheet d. Cleaning schedules

title: Post-Assessment
text: 5 Food Safety in Schools Post-Assessment 21. Which of the following methods of thawing is acceptable? a. Place frozen food in the refrigerator on the bottom shelf. b. Place frozen food in room temperature water. c. Place frozen food in the refrigerator on the top shelf. d. Thaw frozen food in hot water, and then cook immediately. 22. What is the recommended cooking temperature for a raw hamburger patty based on the Food Code? a. 135 °F b. 145 °F c. 155 °F d. 165 °F 23. What are the recommended holding temperatures for cold and hot foods based on the Food Code? a. 41 °F or below for cold foods, 135 °F or above for hot foods b. 45 °F or below for cold foods, 140 °F or above for hot foods c. 41 °F or below for cold foods, 140 °F or above for hot foods d. 45 °F or below for cold foods, 135 °F or above for hot foods 24. Which of the following statements is true about proper cooling of food? a. Plastic containers cool more quickly than stainless steel. b. Putting hot food immediately into a freezer is a best practice. c. Divide food into the smallest quantity possible before cooling. d. Using the two-step cooling method, total time is more important than temperature. 25. Which of the following statements is most true about reheating leftover foods? a. Reheat to 155 °F for 20 seconds. b. Reheat to 165 °F for 20 seconds within 1 hour. c. Reheat to 165 °F for 15 seconds. d. Reheat to 165 °F for 15 seconds within 2 hours.

title: Post-Assessment
text: 6 Post-Assessment Food Safety in Schools 26. Which statement is true about a food safety program based on HACCP principles? a. It is a proactive program to prevent foodborne illness. b. It is a reactive program developed to prevent foodborne illness. c. It is based on results of health department inspections. d. It is designed to be the responsibility of the district school nutrition director. 27. Which approach requires that school nutrition employees group menu items according to the number of times they go through the temperature danger zone? a. HACCP approach b. Process Approach c. Food safety approach d. Cooking approach 28. For no cook items, which steps require temperatures to be checked and recorded? a. Receiving b. Storing c. Cold holding d. All of the above 29. Menu items that go through the temperature danger zone 2-3 times are called: a. no cook items. b. same day service items. c. complex food preparation items. d. cooked items. 30. Menu items such as commercially prepared pizza and chicken nuggets would be categorized as: a. no cook items. b. same day service items. c. complex food preparation items. d. cooked items.
